name: Antergos
description: Antergos was a user-friendly Arch-based distribution that provided a graphical installer and pre-curated desktop choices. Development ended in May 2019; its successor is EndeavourOS.

name: Chakra Linux
description: Chakra was an Arch-based distribution focused on delivering a polished KDE desktop experience with a half-rolling release model. Development ceased in 2013 after several years of declining contributor activity.

name: Bridge Linux
description: Bridge Linux was a lightweight Arch derivative with a friendly installer and a curated set of desktop environments. The project was discontinued in 2018.

name: Arch Linux 32
description: Arch Linux 32 was a community-led port of Arch Linux that provided i686 packages after Arch officially dropped 32-bit support. The project was discontinued in November 2017.

name: SwagArch
description: SwagArch was a community Arch derivative that aimed to provide a polished, opinionated out-of-the-box experience with multiple desktop flavors. It was discontinued in 2018.

name: ArchBang Linux
description: ArchBang was a lightweight Arch-based distribution that provided a minimal Openbox desktop and live ISO for quick deployment. The project went quiet around 2018 with no new releases.

name: BBQLinux
description: BBQLinux was an Arch-based distribution aimed at Android developers, providing the Android SDK and a tuned KDE environment. Development ended in 2019.

name: Converge Linux
description: Converge Linux was a short-lived Arch-based distribution providing pre-curated desktop spins. The project was discontinued after a small number of releases.

name: Evo/Lution
description: Evo/Lution was an Arch-based distribution that packaged a friendly installer with multiple desktop choices. Development was discontinued in 2016.

name: Mimosa Linux
description: Mimosa was a short-lived Arch derivative that provided a curated set of desktop environments. The project was discontinued in 2014.

name: Akita Linux
description: Akita Linux was an Arch-based distribution that offered a graphical installer and a wide variety of desktop environments. The project was discontinued in 2015.

name: Namarupa
description: Namarupa was an Arch derivative aimed at providing a curated GNOME experience. The project was discontinued in 2014.

name: PeBang Linux
description: PeBang was an Arch-based distribution packaged for older 32-bit netbooks and pre-loaded with a MATE desktop. The project was discontinued in 2014.

name: Arch Hurd
description: Arch Hurd was an experimental port of Arch Linux to the GNU Hurd microkernel, packaged with pacman. The project was never completed and has been discontinued.

name: ArchEX
description: ArchEX was an Arch-based live distribution by the same maintainer as Puppy Linux spins, focused on rescue and recovery tasks. The project was discontinued after a number of releases.

name: Apricity OS
description: Apricity OS was a polished Arch-based distribution shipping a Cinnamon desktop with thoughtful defaults. The project was discontinued in 2017 after its founder moved on.

name: CrunchBang Linux
description: CrunchBang was a lightweight Debian-based distribution famous for its Openbox desktop and minimalist tiling workflow. The project was discontinued in 2015; a community fork called CrunchBang++ later emerged.

name: Fuduntu
description: Fuduntu was a Fedora-based distribution aimed at users who preferred a classic GNOME 2-like desktop experience. The project was discontinued in 2013.

name: Sabily
description: Sabily (formerly Ubuntu Muslim Edition) was an Ubuntu derivative that bundled Islamic software, themes, and content filters. The project was discontinued in 2014.

name: Pinguy OS
description: Pinguy OS was an Ubuntu-based distribution focused on easing the transition from Windows or macOS by pre-installing codecs, drivers, and useful software. The project was discontinued in 2017.

name: Linspire
description: Linspire (formerly Lindows) was a Debian/Ubuntu-based distribution marketed at Windows switchers with a familiar interface and bundled commercial applications. The product line was discontinued in 2017.

name: MoonOS
description: MoonOS was an Ubuntu-based distribution notable for its custom GNOME Shell theme and artwork. The project was discontinued in 2011.

name: Ubuntu CE
description: Ubuntu Christian Edition (Ubuntu CE) was an Ubuntu remix that bundled Christian-oriented software, themes, and content filters. The project was discontinued.

name: Ubuntu Lite
description: Ubuntu Lite (originally Linux Lite) was a lightweight Ubuntu remix for older hardware using XFCE and LXDE. The project was discontinued around 2010.

name: Wubi
description: Wubi was an official Windows installer for Ubuntu that placed Ubuntu inside a Windows drive as a loop-mounted image without needing partitioning. The tool was discontinued after Ubuntu 13.04.

name: ExpressGate
description: ExpressGate was an ASUS Linux-based instant-on environment built on a slimmed Ubuntu core, bundled on motherboards and netbooks. The product was phased out in the early 2010s.

name: Flidas
description: Flidas was a short-lived Debian-based distribution that bundled a tailored KDE Plasma desktop. The project was discontinued after a small number of releases.

name: Voyage Linux
description: Voyage Linux was a Debian-based distribution tuned for low-resource devices and 802.11 access points, often deployed on embedded hardware. The project was discontinued in 2017.

name: gNewSense
description: gNewSense was a Debian-based distribution endorsed by the Free Software Foundation that removed all non-free components and shipped only the Linux-libre kernel. The project was discontinued in 2010, with users directed toward Trisquel.

name: Hanthana Linux
description: Hanthana was a Sri Lankan Fedora remix that bundled popular multimedia codecs and fonts for Sinhala users. The project was discontinued around 2015.

name: Korora Project
description: Korora was a Fedora-based distribution that aimed to provide a friendly out-of-the-box experience with codecs, drivers, and several desktop spins. The project was discontinued in 2017.

name: Poseidon Linux
description: Poseidon Linux was a Debian-based distribution focused on scientific research and academia, bundling tools for data analysis. The project was discontinued in 2014.

name: Damn Vulnerable Linux
description: Damn Vulnerable Linux (DVL) was a Debian-based live distribution designed to teach security and exploitation techniques against a deliberately insecure system. The project was discontinued.

name: Samurai Web Testing Framework
description: Samurai was an Ubuntu-based live distribution that pre-installed a suite of open-source web application penetration testing tools. The project was discontinued in 2016.

name: Santoku Linux
description: Santoku was an Ubuntu-based distribution that provided a toolkit for mobile forensics, security, and penetration testing. The project was discontinued in 2016.

name: Security Auditor
description: Security Auditor was a Debian-based live distribution aimed at security assessment, reverse engineering, and forensic analysis. The project was eventually discontinued.

name: BlackBuntu
description: BlackBuntu was an Ubuntu-based penetration testing distribution positioned as an alternative to BackTrack. The project was discontinued in the early 2010s.

name: WeakNet Linux
description: WeakNet Linux was a Debian-based distribution aimed at security research and penetration testing, often produced as a companion to the WeakNet Labs training courses. The project was discontinued.

name: NodeZero
description: NodeZero was an Ubuntu-based live distribution that bundled penetration testing and forensic tools into a single image. The project was discontinued in the early 2010s.

name: Mempo
description: Mempo was a privacy-focused Debian-based distribution that bundled hardened browser bundles and Tor tools. The project was discontinued around 2014.

name: Freedows
description: Freedows was a Brazilian Debian-based distribution produced in the early 2000s with a friendly installer and Portuguese localization. The project was discontinued in 2001.

name: Dragon Linux
description: Dragon Linux was a short-lived Debian-based distribution that paired a custom installer with KDE. The project was discontinued in the early 2000s.

name: Hiweed GNU/Linux
description: Hiweed GNU/Linux was a Chinese Debian-based distribution that combined an easy installer with XFCE and GNOME environments. The project was discontinued.

name: Yellow Dog Linux
description: Yellow Dog Linux was a Fedora/Red Hat-derived distribution primarily for PowerPC hardware, including PlayStation 3 and older Macs. The project was discontinued in 2012.

name: Fedora Unity
description: Fedora Unity was a Fedora community respin that re-integrated non-free drivers and codecs removed by Fedora's stricter default policy. The project was discontinued in 2009.

name: Moblin
description: Moblin (Mobile Linux) was an Intel-led Fedora-based project for netbooks, MIDs, and embedded devices, which later merged with Maemo to form MeeGo. The Moblin project was discontinued in 2010.

name: Scientific Linux
description: Scientific Linux was a Red Hat Enterprise Linux rebuild produced by Fermilab and CERN for scientific computing. The project was discontinued in 2019, with users directed toward CentOS and Rocky Linux.

name: CentOS Linux
description: CentOS Linux was a community rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux widely used on production servers. The project ended in 2021, with CentOS Stream replacing it as the upstream development branch.

name: Red Hat Linux
description: Red Hat Linux was the original free, community Red Hat distribution that was discontinued in 2004 and replaced by Red Hat Enterprise Linux. Fedora Core (later Fedora) emerged as the community successor.

name: Navy Linux
description: Navy Linux was a community rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux focused on stability and long-term support. The project was discontinued in 2021 following Red Hat's CentOS changes.

name: ClearOS
description: ClearOS was a RHEL-derivative small business server distribution that combined gateway, mail, and file sharing roles into a single appliance. The project was discontinued in 2021.

name: Chinese Fedora
description: Chinese Fedora was a Chinese-localized Fedora remix with input methods, fonts, and tutorials aimed at Chinese desktop users. The project was discontinued.

name: RISI
description: RISI was a Fedora-based distribution that provided a polished, opinionated KDE experience with carefully selected defaults. The project was discontinued in the early 2010s.

name: Slackintosh
description: Slackintosh was a Slackware-based distribution specifically maintained for PowerPC and early Intel Macs. The project was discontinued in 2012.

name: ZipSlack
description: ZipSlack was a minimal Slackware distribution that ran from a single zip file on a Windows FAT partition. The project was discontinued.

name: Bluewhite64
description: Bluewhite64 was a 64-bit-only Slackware derivative providing current packages and a 64-bit Linux kernel. The project was discontinued in 2011.

name: Absolute Linux
description: Absolute Linux was a Slackware-based distribution that bundled a curated KDE or XFCE desktop with extra multimedia and font packages. The project was discontinued in 2020.

name: Nanolinux
description: Nanolinux was a tiny Slackware-based distribution that could fit on a 64MB USB stick and boot into a graphical XFCE environment. The project was discontinued in 2016.

name: rPath Linux
description: rPath Linux was a Slackware-inspired distribution that used the Conary package manager for transactional updates. The project was discontinued in 2012 as the company shifted to appliance management.

name: Sabayon Linux
description: Sabayon was a Gentoo-based distribution that provided a binary-compatible user-friendly experience with the Entropy package manager. The project was discontinued in 2020, with developer attention shifting to Funtoo Continuum.

name: Mandriva Linux
description: Mandriva Linux (formerly Mandrake Linux) was a French distribution known for its polished installer and friendly defaults. The company went bankrupt in 2012, ending development of the distribution.

name: Mandrake Linux
description: Mandrake Linux was an early Red Hat-derived distribution targeted at desktop users. It was renamed to Mandriva in 2005; the original brand was discontinued.

name: Conectiva
description: Conectiva was a Brazilian Portuguese-localized distribution that merged with Mandriva in 2005. The Conectiva brand was discontinued in 2011 after the parent company collapsed.

name: Rosa Fresh
description: ROSA Fresh was a community Mandriva-derived distribution produced by ROSA Lab. The community edition was discontinued in 2017 after commercial priorities shifted.

name: Sabotage Linux
description: Sabotage was a minimalist Linux distribution built from Linux from Scratch with a custom package manager called butch. Development slowed and the project was effectively discontinued around 2018.

name: Lunar Linux
description: Lunar Linux was a source-based distribution forked from Sorcerer, providing the custom 'lin' package manager. The project was discontinued in 2016 after years of declining activity.

name: exGENT
description: exGENT was a Gentoo-based live distribution by Arne Exton that combined current Linux kernel builds with a curated desktop. The project was discontinued in 2015.

name: Oryx Linux
description: Oryx Linux was a Slackware-based distribution that provided a polished KDE experience. The project was discontinued in 2012.

name: Bluewall Linux
description: Bluewall was a CentOS-based distribution that bundled security tools and was designed to be a stable platform for network and system administration. The project was discontinued.

name: Dyne:bolic
description: Dyne:bolic was a multimedia production live distribution for audio, video, and graphics work, popular among activists and artists. The project was discontinued in 2018.

name: Shark Linux
description: Shark Linux was a small rescue and recovery live distribution with a curated set of disk and network tools. The project was discontinued in 2014.

name: Parted Magic
description: Parted Magic was a small commercial Linux live distribution focused on disk partitioning, cloning, and data recovery. The project was effectively discontinued after the 2023 releases.

name: Hiren's Boot CD
description: Hiren's Boot CD was a multiboot rescue disk that bundled Windows PE, Mini Linux, and DOS tools for diagnostics and recovery. The Linux-only edition (PE edition followed) was discontinued in 2012.

name: Yggdrasil Linux/GNU/X
description: Yggdrasil Linux/GNU/X was widely regarded as the first Linux distribution, created by Adam Engel and released in 1992 as a single floppy-based CD. The project was discontinued in 1997.

name: Caldera OpenLinux
description: Caldera OpenLinux was a commercial Linux distribution produced by Caldera Systems in the late 1990s. The product was discontinued in 2002 after the company merged with SCO.

name: Turbolinux
description: Turbolinux was a Japanese commercial Linux distribution popular in East Asia for desktop and server use. The product was discontinued in 2009.

name: Storm Linux
description: Storm Linux was an early 2000s Linux distribution that used the Storm Package Manager and was later acquired by Mandriva. The product was discontinued in 2000 after the acquisition.

name: ArtistX
description: ArtistX was an Ubuntu-based live DVD that pre-installed a wide range of free multimedia creation tools for audio, video, 2D, and 3D artists. The project was discontinued in 2012.

name: Rocks Cluster Distribution
description: Rocks Cluster Distribution was a Red Hat/CentOS-based distribution that simplified HPC cluster deployment through a roll-based installer. The project was discontinued in 2016.

name: SolusOS
description: SolusOS was a Debian-based distribution by the lead developer of the original Solus project before the EvolveOS/Solus rework. The project was discontinued in 2013.

name: Linpus Linux
description: Linpus Linux was a Linux distribution popular as the pre-installed OS on Acer and Lenovo netbooks in the late 2000s. The original Linpus desktop product was effectively discontinued by 2018.
